algorithm.name: Top-K with Min-Heap
algorithm.description: Maintain a min-heap of exactly K elements. For each of N items, push if heap size < K; otherwise compare with heap minimum and swap if new item is larger. Final heap contains top-K.

input.description: Stream of N items each with a comparable timestamp

output.description: K items with the largest timestamps
properties: Items are globally top-K by timestamp

dataStructure.name: Binary Min-Heap
dataStructure.description: Fixed-capacity heap of size K tracking the K largest timestamps seen so far

operations.name: peek-min
operations.description: Inspect smallest timestamp in current top-K set

operations.name: push
operations.description: Insert new item and sift up to restore heap property

operations.name: pop-min
operations.description: Remove root (smallest item) and sift down

alternatives.name: Full Sort then slice
alternatives.reason: Sorts all N items including those that will never appear in output; wasteful when K << N

alternatives.name: Quickselect (randomized)
alternatives.reason: Finds K-th largest in expected O(N) but does not return sorted top-K; poor worst-case without median-of-medians

alternatives.name: Max-Heap over all N items
alternatives.reason: Heapify in O(N) then extract-max K times; better when K > N / log N but uses O(N) space

comparison.justification: For K=100 and N=1M, O(N log K) = O(7N) vs. O(N log N) = O(20N) for full sort — roughly 3x fewer comparisons. Space usage is O(K)=O(100) vs. O(N)=O(1M), critical when items are large objects. Quickselect has better asymptotic time but requires modification to return all K items in order.

assumptions: K is known in advance and is small relative to N
Items can be processed one at a time (streaming acceptable)
keyInsight: A min-heap lets you evict the smallest of your current top-K candidates in O(log K) per item — you never need to see the full dataset at once and never sort more elements than necessary.
